note: Practice questions authored by Fly GACA from the GACAR library for study. Not actual GACA exam questions. Every answer cites its source — verify against the official GACAR.

banks.title: Airline Transport Pilot Licensing
banks.desc: ATP certificate eligibility, aeronautical experience, privileges and limitations (GACAR Part 61, Subpart G).
banks.source: GACAR Part 61, Subpart G · DRAFT — pending review

questions.q: Which pilot certificate is governed by GACAR Part 61, Subpart G?
questions.explain: GACAR §61.171 states that Subpart G prescribes the requirements for the issuance of airline transport pilot (ATP) certificates and ratings, the conditions under which they are necessary, and their privileges and limitations.

questions.q: Under GACAR Part 61, the minimum age to be eligible for an airline transport pilot (ATP) certificate is —
questions.explain: GACAR §61.173(a) requires an ATP certificate applicant to be at least 23 years of age.

questions.q: Which class of medical certificate must an applicant generally hold to exercise the privileges of an ATP certificate?
questions.explain: GACAR §61.173(i) requires an ATP applicant to hold a Class 1 medical certificate (with a limited glider/balloon exception permitting a Class 2).

questions.q: For an ATP certificate with an airplane category and class rating, what is the minimum total time as a pilot required?
questions.explain: GACAR §61.181(a) requires at least 1,500 hours of total time as a pilot for an ATP certificate with an airplane category and class rating.

questions.q: The aeronautical experience for an ATP airplane rating must include at least how many hours of cross-country flight time?
questions.explain: GACAR §61.181(a)(1) requires at least 500 hours of cross-country flight time within the 1,500-hour total for an ATP airplane rating.

questions.q: How many hours of night flight time are required for an ATP certificate with an airplane category and class rating?
questions.explain: GACAR §61.181(a)(2) requires at least 100 hours of night flight time for an ATP airplane rating.

questions.q: The ATP airplane aeronautical experience requirement includes at least how many hours of instrument flight time in actual or simulated instrument conditions?
questions.explain: GACAR §61.181(a)(4) requires at least 75 hours of instrument flight time in actual or simulated instrument conditions for an ATP airplane rating.

questions.q: For an ATP airplane rating, at least how many hours of flight time must be as pilot in command (or as SIC performing PIC duties under supervision)?
questions.explain: GACAR §61.181(a)(5) requires at least 250 hours of flight time in an airplane as PIC, or as SIC performing the duties of a PIC under supervision, for an ATP airplane rating.

questions.q: For an ATP certificate with a rotorcraft category and helicopter class rating, what is the minimum total time as a pilot required?
questions.explain: GACAR §61.183(a) requires at least 1,200 hours of total time as a pilot for an ATP certificate with a rotorcraft category and helicopter class rating.

questions.q: For an ATP certificate with a powered-lift category rating, what is the minimum total time as a pilot required?
questions.explain: GACAR §61.185(a) requires at least 1,500 hours of total time as a pilot for an ATP certificate with a powered-lift category rating.

questions.q: In multi-pilot international operations under GACAR Part 121 or 135, a pilot holding an ATP certificate may not serve as a pilot after attaining which birthday?
questions.explain: GACAR §61.3(c) bars a commercial or ATP certificate holder from serving as a pilot in international Part 121/135 operations after the 60th birthday, or the 65th birthday in the case of operations with more than one pilot.

questions.q: Under GACAR §61.175, when is an applicant NOT required to pass a knowledge test to add an aircraft type rating to an ATP certificate?
options: When the ATP certificate already lists the aircraft category and class rating appropriate to the type rating sought
Never — a knowledge test is always required
When the applicant is over 40 years of age
When the applicant holds a foreign private pilot licence
questions.explain: GACAR §61.175(b) waives the knowledge test for an additional aircraft type rating when the applicant's ATP certificate already lists the aircraft category and class rating appropriate to the type rating sought.

questions.q: The ATP certification training program for an airplane multiengine class rating requires at least how many hours of academic (classroom) instruction?
questions.explain: GACAR §61.177(a) requires at least 30 hours of classroom instruction in the ATP certification training program for an airplane multiengine class rating.

questions.q: Within the ATP certification training program (airplane multiengine), how many hours of flight-simulation training device (FSTD) training are required?
questions.explain: GACAR §61.177(b) requires at least 10 hours of training in an FSTD representing a multiengine turbine airplane as part of the ATP certification training program.

questions.q: For an ATP airplane rating, at least how many hours must be flown in the specific class of airplane for the rating sought?
questions.explain: GACAR §61.181(a)(3) requires at least 50 hours of flight time in the class of airplane for the rating sought (with limited FFS credit).

questions.q: For an ATP rotorcraft/helicopter rating, the 100 hours of required night flight time must include at least how many hours in helicopters?
questions.explain: GACAR §61.183(a)(2) requires 100 hours of night flight time, of which at least 15 hours are in helicopters, for an ATP rotorcraft/helicopter rating.

questions.q: Which of the following is a valid eligibility path to an ATP certificate under GACAR §61.173?
options: Holding a commercial pilot certificate with an instrument rating
Holding a private pilot certificate only
questions.explain: GACAR §61.173(c) requires the applicant to hold a commercial pilot certificate with an instrument rating (or a qualifying foreign ATP licence with instrument privileges).

questions.q: For an ATP rotorcraft/helicopter rating, at least how many hours of cross-country flight time are required?
questions.explain: GACAR §61.183(a)(1) requires at least 500 hours of cross-country flight time for an ATP certificate with a rotorcraft category and helicopter class rating.

questions.q: For an ATP powered-lift rating, at least how many hours must be flown in a powered-lift as PIC (or as SIC performing PIC duties under supervision)?
questions.explain: GACAR §61.185(a)(3) requires at least 250 hours in a powered-lift as PIC, or as SIC performing the duties of a PIC under supervision, for an ATP powered-lift rating.

questions.q: For the ATP airplane rating, not more than how many hours of the total aeronautical experience may be obtained in a full flight simulator or flight training device (in an approved course)?
questions.explain: GACAR §61.181(a)(6) allows not more than 100 hours of the total aeronautical experience to be obtained in a full flight simulator or flight training device representing an airplane, when accomplished in an approved Part 121/135/141/142 course.

questions.q: Besides the minimum age, which is an eligibility requirement for an ATP certificate under GACAR §61.173?
options: Be currently employed by an airline
questions.explain: GACAR §61.173(b) requires an ATP certificate applicant to be of good moral character, in addition to the age and other requirements.

questions.q: To be eligible for an ATP certificate with an airplane multiengine class rating, GACAR §61.173(d) requires the applicant to present —
options: A graduation certificate for the ATP certification training program (§61.177)
A university degree in aeronautical engineering
A type rating in a widebody aircraft
questions.explain: GACAR §61.173(d) requires, for an airplane multiengine class ATP rating, a graduation certificate from an authorized training provider certifying completion of the ATP certification training program specified in §61.177 before taking the knowledge test.

banks.title: Air Transport Operations
banks.desc: Operating rules for transport-category commercial air operators under GACAR Part 121.

questions.q: GACAR Part 121 prescribes rules governing the scheduled and unscheduled operations of a commercial air operator operating which category of aircraft?
options: Transport category airplanes/rotorcraft or commuter category airplanes
questions.explain: GACAR §121.1 applies Part 121 to commercial air operators operating transport category airplanes, transport category rotorcraft, or commuter category airplanes that hold or must hold an Air Operator Certificate.

questions.q: Under GACAR Part 121, a certificate holder must hold or be required to hold an Air Operator Certificate (AOC) issued under which GACAR Part?
questions.explain: GACAR §121.1 ties Part 121 operations to holding an Air Operator Certificate under GACAR Part 119; a certificate holder is defined as a Part 119 AOC holder operating under this part.

questions.q: For the purposes of GACAR Part 121, the term 'special unscheduled' distinguishes requirements applicable to aircraft with a type-certificated passenger seating capacity of —
questions.explain: GACAR §121.5 uses 'special unscheduled' for unscheduled operations applicable only to aircraft with a type-certificated passenger seating capacity of 56 seats or less, excluding required crew members.

questions.q: For scheduled and unscheduled operations, which of the following is NOT one of the full-time management positions a Part 121 certificate holder must staff?
questions.explain: GACAR §121.45(a) requires a director of safety, director of operations, chief pilot, director of maintenance, and chief inspector; a director of training is not among the listed required positions.

questions.q: Under GACAR §121.45, a certificate holder must notify the President of any change in required management personnel or any vacancy within how many working days?
questions.explain: GACAR §121.45(f)(3) requires the certificate holder to notify the President within 10 working days of any change in personnel or any vacancy in a listed position.

questions.q: A certificate holder conducting only special unscheduled operations must staff which management positions under GACAR §121.45?
options: Director of operations, chief pilot, and director of maintenance
Director of safety and chief inspector only
Chief pilot and chief inspector only
All five positions required for scheduled operations
questions.explain: GACAR §121.45(b) requires special unscheduled operators to have a director of operations, a chief pilot, and a director of maintenance — a reduced set compared with scheduled operations.

questions.q: To serve as director of operations for a Part 121 certificate holder, a person must hold at minimum which pilot certificate?
questions.explain: GACAR §121.49(a) requires the director of operations to hold an airline transport pilot (ATP) certificate, plus specified supervisory and PIC experience.

questions.q: Under GACAR §121.49, to serve as chief inspector a person must hold a mechanic certificate with both airframe and powerplant ratings and have held those ratings for at least —
questions.explain: GACAR §121.49(d) requires the chief inspector to hold a mechanic certificate with airframe and powerplant ratings and to have held those ratings for at least 3 years.

questions.q: Under GACAR §121.13, if a certificate holder knowingly permits an owned or leased aircraft to be engaged in an operation violating the psychoactive-substances rule, that operation is a basis for —
questions.explain: GACAR §121.13 provides that permitting an aircraft to be used in violation of GACAR §91.23 (carriage of psychoactive substances) is a basis for suspending or revoking the certificate.

questions.q: Beyond the general Part 91 documents, GACAR §121.17 requires a Part 121 certificate holder to carry on board —
options: A true copy of the AOC and a copy of the operations specifications
A copy of the certificate holder's financial statements
The original AOC and original operations specifications only
questions.explain: GACAR §121.17 requires carrying a true copy of the AOC and a copy of the operations specifications (and, when applicable, a true copy of the Article 83bis agreement).

questions.q: Under GACAR §121.139, each Part 121 certificate holder must prepare and keep current which manuals?
options: An operations manual and a maintenance manual
A marketing manual and a safety manual
questions.explain: GACAR §121.139(a) requires the certificate holder to prepare and keep current both an operations manual and a maintenance manual for the use and guidance of appropriate personnel.

questions.q: Among the requirements of GACAR §121.139, each required manual must —
options: Be available in the English language
Be revised no more than once per year
questions.explain: GACAR §121.139(c) requires each manual to be available in the English language, be easy to revise, reference applicable GACAR, and include human factors principles.

questions.q: GACAR §121.143 lists the content areas that the operations manual must contain. Which of the following is NOT one of them?
questions.explain: GACAR §121.143(a) organizes operations-manual contents under General; Aircraft operating information; Areas, routes, and aerodromes; and Training — revenue accounting is not a required content area.

questions.q: Under GACAR §121.147, a certificate holder must keep a current approved Aircraft Flight Manual (AFM), or approved equivalent, for each type of aircraft —
options: At its principal base of operations and in each aircraft of that type
Only in the certificate holder's legal department
Only aboard the aircraft during international flights
questions.explain: GACAR §121.147(a) requires a current approved AFM or approved equivalent at the principal base of operations and in each aircraft of that type that the holder operates.

questions.q: Under GACAR §121.151, copies of the required manual (or appropriate parts) must be furnished to all of the following EXCEPT —
options: Appropriate ground operations and maintenance personnel
The GACA representatives assigned to it
questions.explain: GACAR §121.151(a) requires furnishing manuals to ground operations and maintenance personnel, crew members, and assigned GACA representatives; passengers are not on that list.

questions.q: Under GACAR §121.517, no person may take off an aircraft with inoperable instruments or equipment installed unless —
options: The PIC gives verbal approval before departure
An approved minimum equipment list (MEL) exists and the operations specifications authorize operations under it
The item is scheduled for repair within 10 days
A passenger briefing on the defect is given
questions.explain: GACAR §121.517(a) permits takeoff with inoperable items only when an approved MEL exists and the operations specifications authorize operation under that MEL, with the aircraft operated under its conditions and limitations.

questions.q: Which of the following may NOT be included in a certificate holder's approved MEL under GACAR §121.517?
options: Instruments or equipment required by an airworthiness directive to be operable
questions.explain: GACAR §121.517(b) bars the MEL from including instruments/equipment required by an airworthiness directive to be operable, items required by the type-certification airworthiness requirements, and items required for specific operations by Part 121 and Part 91.

questions.q: Under GACAR §121.1301, a certificate holder conducting scheduled operations must have which operational-control system?
questions.explain: GACAR §121.1301 requires scheduled operators to have a dispatch release system; unscheduled operators may use either a flight release system or a dispatch release system.

questions.q: Under the dispatch release system of GACAR §121.1309, no person may continue a flight from an intermediate aerodrome without re-dispatch if the aircraft has been on the ground more than —
questions.explain: GACAR §121.1309(a) prohibits continuing a flight from an intermediate aerodrome without re-dispatch once the aircraft has been on the ground more than 6 hours.

questions.q: Under the dispatch release system of GACAR §121.1309, who are jointly responsible for the preflight planning, delay, and dispatch release of a flight?
options: The PIC and the aircraft dispatcher
The chief pilot and the director of safety
The GACA and the certificate holder
The first officer and the cabin crew
questions.explain: GACAR §121.1309(a)(4) makes the PIC and the aircraft dispatcher jointly responsible for the preflight planning, delay, and dispatch release, in compliance with Part 121, the operations specifications, and the manual.

questions.q: Under the flight release system of GACAR §121.1325, operational control for each flight must be shared between the PIC and —
questions.explain: GACAR §121.1325(a) requires operational control for each flight to be shared between the PIC and the director of operations, who may delegate that authority as specified in the manual.

questions.q: Under GACAR §121.1001, a Part 121 certificate holder may satisfy flight-crew flight-time and duty limitations either by complying with GACAR Part 117 or by implementing —
options: A fatigue risk management system (FRMS) providing an equivalent level of safety
A doubled minimum rest period only
No alternative — Part 117 is the sole option
questions.explain: GACAR §121.1001 lets a certificate holder either comply with the Part 117 flight-time and duty limitations or implement a comprehensive FRMS, approved by the President, that provides an equivalent level of safety.

banks.title: Transport Performance & Dispatch
banks.desc: Performance, weather minima and dispatch requirements for transport-category operations (GACAR Part 121).
banks.source: GACAR Part 121 (performance & dispatch) · DRAFT — pending review

questions.q: Which subpart of GACAR Part 121 prescribes airplane performance operating limitations for all certificate holders?
questions.explain: Subpart F prescribes airplane performance operating limitations; Subpart G covers rotorcraft performance, Subpart M covers dispatcher qualifications, and Subpart Q covers records and reports.

questions.q: Under GACAR §121.263, in computing takeoff distance the length of any clearway that may be included must not be greater than —
options: One-quarter the length of the runway
One-half the length of the runway
The full length of the runway
One-and-a-half times the length of the runway
questions.explain: §121.263 provides that the takeoff distance must not exceed the runway plus any clearway, but the clearway credited must not be greater than one-half the length of the runway.

questions.q: Under the destination landing limitations of GACAR §121.275, a turbojet-powered airplane's mass at takeoff must allow a full-stop landing within what portion of the effective runway length at the destination?
questions.explain: §121.275 requires that the anticipated landing mass allow a full-stop landing within 60 percent of the effective length of the runway (measured from 50 ft above the runway) for turbojet airplanes at the destination.

questions.q: Under GACAR §121.275, if weather reports indicate the destination runways may be wet or slippery, a turbojet airplane may take off only if the effective runway length is at least what percentage of the dry-runway length otherwise required?
questions.explain: §121.275 requires that, for wet or slippery destination runways, the effective runway length be at least 115 percent of the length required for dry conditions, unless a shorter approved wet distance is in the AFM.

questions.q: Under GACAR §121.279, an aerodrome may be listed as an alternate for a turbopropeller-powered airplane only if the aircraft can be brought to a full stop within what portion of the effective runway length?
questions.explain: §121.279 permits listing an alternate for a turbopropeller airplane only if a full-stop landing can be made within 70 percent of the effective runway length (60 percent for turbojet airplanes).

questions.q: GACAR §121.271 (en-route limitations, two engines inoperative) allows compliance by ensuring no point along the intended track is more than how long — with all engines operating at cruising power — from an adequate aerodrome?
questions.explain: One compliance option in §121.271 is that no place along the intended track is more than 90 minutes (all engines at cruising power) from an aerodrome meeting §121.279.

questions.q: Under the en-route limitation for one engine inoperative (GACAR §121.267), the airplane's mass must permit a net flight path clearing terrain and obstructions within the specified corridor by at least —
questions.explain: §121.267 requires (for the option reaching a landing aerodrome) that the one-engine-inoperative net flight path clear all terrain and obstructions within 4.4 NM of track by at least 2,000 ft vertically.

questions.q: Under GACAR §121.1309, no flight under the dispatch release system may start unless —
options: The pilot-in-command alone signs the load manifest
An aircraft dispatcher specifically authorizes that flight
The airplane has departed within the previous hour
questions.explain: §121.1309 provides that no person may start a flight unless an aircraft dispatcher specifically authorizes it, and the PIC and dispatcher are jointly responsible for preflight planning and dispatch release.

questions.q: Under GACAR §121.1309, a flight may not continue from an intermediate aerodrome without a re-dispatch if the aircraft has been on the ground more than —
questions.explain: §121.1309 requires re-dispatch to continue a flight from an intermediate aerodrome when the aircraft has been on the ground more than 6 hours.

questions.q: Under GACAR §121.1369, an aircraft may be dispatched or released for operations under IFR or over-the-top only when appropriate weather reports or forecasts indicate that the weather at the destination will be —
options: At or above the authorized minimums at the estimated time of arrival
VFR at all times en route
questions.explain: §121.1369 permits IFR/over-the-top dispatch or release only when weather reports or forecasts indicate conditions will be at or above the authorized minimums at the estimated time of arrival at the destination.

questions.q: Under GACAR §121.1393, a flight conducted under IFR or over-the-top must generally have how many destination alternate aerodromes specified in the dispatch or flight release?
options: None if the flight is under 3 hours
One alternate only for extended over-water flights
questions.explain: §121.1393 requires at least one destination alternate aerodrome be selected and specified for IFR or over-the-top flights, subject to limited exceptions (e.g., favorable forecast ceiling/visibility windows or isolated aerodromes).

questions.q: Under GACAR §121.1397, no aerodrome may be listed as an alternate in a dispatch or flight release unless the appropriate weather reports or forecasts indicate the weather will be —
options: At or above the alternate weather minimums in the operations specifications when the flight arrives
Below landing minimums but with an ILS available
questions.explain: §121.1397 permits listing an alternate only when the weather is forecast to be at or above the alternate weather minimums in the certificate holder's operations specifications at the time the flight arrives.

questions.q: Under GACAR §121.1389, when a takeoff alternate is required, for an airplane having two engines the takeoff alternate must be located no more than —
options: 1 hour from the departure aerodrome at one-engine-inoperative cruise speed
2 hours from the departure aerodrome at all-engine cruise speed
questions.explain: §121.1389 requires a two-engine airplane's takeoff alternate to be within 1 hour of the departure aerodrome at the normal one-engine-inoperative cruising speed (ISA, still air, actual takeoff mass); three-or-more-engine airplanes get 2 hours at all-engine cruise.

questions.q: Under GACAR §121.1365, an aircraft may be dispatched or released for VFR operation only if the ceiling and visibility en route —
options: Are forecast to improve after departure
Are and will remain at or above applicable VFR minimums until arrival at the specified aerodrome(s)
Are reported at the departure aerodrome only
questions.explain: §121.1365 permits VFR dispatch or release only when weather reports or forecasts show the ceiling and visibility en route are and will remain at or above applicable VFR minimums until the aircraft reaches the aerodrome(s) specified.

questions.q: Under GACAR §121.1349, before releasing (or beginning) a flight, the aircraft dispatcher or PIC must —
options: Be thoroughly familiar with reported and forecast weather conditions on the route to be flown
File a written risk assessment with the President
questions.explain: §121.1349 requires the aircraft dispatcher (dispatch release system) or the PIC (flight release system) to be thoroughly familiar with reported and forecast weather conditions on the route to be flown before releasing/beginning the flight.

questions.q: Under GACAR §121.1341, a flight may not be allowed to continue to the aerodrome to which it was released unless the weather at a specified alternate is forecast to be —
options: At or above the alternate minimums in the operations specifications at the time the aircraft would arrive there
Clear of clouds regardless of visibility
The same as the departure aerodrome
questions.explain: §121.1341 prohibits continuing a flight unless the weather at a specified alternate is forecast to be at or above the alternate minimums in the operations specifications when the aircraft would arrive; the release may be amended en route to add an alternate within fuel range.

questions.q: GACAR §121.89 addresses ETOPS communications facilities. What does it primarily require of the certificate holder?
options: Provide voice communications for ETOPS where such facilities are available, considering diversion routes and altitudes
Install a second HF radio in every airplane
Restrict ETOPS to daylight hours only
Carry a satellite phone for each passenger
questions.explain: §121.89 requires the certificate holder to provide voice communications for ETOPS where facilities are available, considering potential routes and altitudes needed for diversion to ETOPS alternate aerodromes, substituting an approved system where voice is not possible.

questions.q: Under GACAR §121.1401, an aircraft may be dispatched or released for an ETOPS flight only if —
options: The destination weather is forecast VFR
Enough ETOPS alternate aerodromes are listed so the aircraft remains within the authorized ETOPS maximum diversion time
Every alternate has a precision approach
questions.explain: §121.1401 requires that enough ETOPS alternate aerodromes be listed in the dispatch or flight release so the aircraft remains within the authorized ETOPS maximum diversion time, selecting from adequate aerodromes meeting the standards of the part.

questions.q: Under GACAR §121.1409, for ETOPS up to and including 180 minutes, an aerodrome may not be listed as an ETOPS alternate if the time needed to reach it exceeds the aircraft's most limiting ETOPS significant system time minus —
questions.explain: §121.1409 provides that, for ETOPS up to 180 minutes, an aerodrome cannot be an ETOPS alternate if the flight time to it (one-engine-inoperative cruise, standard conditions) exceeds the most limiting ETOPS significant system time minus 15 minutes.

questions.q: Under the ETOPS fuel-supply rule (GACAR §121.1417), the aircraft must carry fuel to hold at 1,500 ft above field elevation upon reaching an ETOPS alternate for how long, then conduct an approach and land?
questions.explain: §121.1417 requires ETOPS fuel sufficient to hold at 1,500 ft above field elevation for 15 minutes upon reaching an ETOPS alternate aerodrome and then conduct an instrument approach and land, in addition to diversion, wind, icing, and deterioration allowances.

questions.q: Under GACAR §121.1417, when calculating ETOPS diversion fuel the certificate holder must increase the forecast wind speed by what margin to account for wind-forecasting errors?
questions.explain: §121.1417 requires the actual forecast wind speed to be increased by 5 percent (increasing headwind or decreasing tailwind) — or additional reserve fuel equal to 5 percent of the diversion fuel — to account for potential wind-forecasting errors.

banks.title: General Air Law
banks.desc: The structure of the GACARs, the regulator, and how Saudi aviation law fits together.

questions.q: The civil aviation regulator of the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia is —
options: The General Authority of Civil Aviation (GACA)
questions.explain: The General Authority of Civil Aviation (GACA) is the State's civil aviation regulator and publishes the GACARs.

questions.q: Which GACAR Part contains the general operating and flight rules?
questions.explain: GACAR Part 91 — General Operating and Flight Rules — governs the operation of aircraft within KSA.

questions.q: Pilot certification and licensing requirements are set out in which GACAR Part?
questions.explain: GACAR Part 61 covers the certification of airmen — pilot licences, ratings and the associated requirements.

questions.q: The GACARs are written to comply, as far as practicable, with —
options: The Standards and Recommended Practices of ICAO
questions.explain: The stated goal of the GACAR is to comply, to the maximum extent practicable, with ICAO Standards and Recommended Practices; differences are published in the AIP.

questions.q: The GACAR Parts are numbered in the range —
questions.explain: The GACARs run from Part 1 through Part 199, grouped by subject area.

questions.q: A pilot holding a foreign (e.g. FAA or EASA) licence who wishes to fly on the Saudi register must —
options: Use the foreign licence directly with no further action
Convert it to, or obtain, a GACA licence
Only fly with a Saudi co-pilot
questions.explain: A foreign licence must be converted to — or a GACA licence otherwise obtained — to exercise privileges on the Saudi register.

questions.q: May a pilot in command deviate from a rule of GACAR Part 91 in an in-flight emergency?
options: Yes — to the extent required to meet that emergency
questions.explain: GACAR §91.3 allows the PIC, in an in-flight emergency requiring immediate action, to deviate from the rules to the extent required to meet that emergency.

questions.q: The authoritative, current source for any GACAR requirement is —
questions.explain: Only GACA's official publication is authoritative. Study tools and summaries must always be verified against the current official GACAR.

questions.q: Where ICAO Annex provisions are adopted into Saudi law, they take effect —
options: When adopted by reference within the GACARs
Only after a NOTAM is issued
questions.explain: ICAO Annex provisions apply in KSA where the GACARs adopt them — often by reference within a relevant Part.

questions.q: The topical handbook that explains the legal basis, ICAO compliance and scope of the GACARs is the —
questions.explain: The Foreword sets out the legal basis, historical background, ICAO compliance and applicability of the GACARs.

questions.q: Amendments to the GACARs and their effective dates are tracked in the —
questions.explain: The Change History document records GACAR amendments and the version in which each change took effect.

questions.q: GACAR Part 91 applies to aircraft operations —
options: Within the sovereign area and territorial waters of KSA
questions.explain: GACAR §91.1 — Part 91 prescribes rules governing the operation of aircraft within the sovereign area and territorial waters of the Kingdom.

questions.q: Which GACAR Part prescribes the rules governing the issuance, amendment and repeal of the GACARs?
questions.explain: GACAR §11.1 states that Part 11 prescribes the rules governing the issuance, amendment and repeal of the GACARs and the requirements for petitions for exemption.

questions.q: Under GACAR Part 11, who may petition the President for an exemption from all or part of a regulatory requirement?
options: Only the GACA Board of Directors
questions.explain: GACAR §11.9 provides that any person may petition the President for an exemption from all or part of a regulatory requirement issued by the GACA.

questions.q: If a petition for exemption is denied, the petitioner may next file —
options: A petition for reconsideration with the President
A new application after five years
questions.explain: GACAR §11.13 allows a petitioner whose exemption was denied to file a petition for reconsideration with the President, based on an erroneous material fact or new relevant facts.

questions.q: Under GACAR §13.23, a person who knows of a violation of the Civil Aviation Law or a rule issued under it must —
questions.explain: GACAR §13.23 requires any person who knows of a violation of the Civil Aviation Law or any rule, regulation or order under it to report it to the President.

questions.q: Which remedial administrative action may the President issue to bring to a person's attention that an incident appears contrary to the regulations but does not warrant further enforcement?
questions.explain: GACAR §13.63 allows the President to issue a warning notice when the facts appear contrary to the regulations but do not warrant further compliance enforcement action.

questions.q: As a result of an investigation, the President may issue an order amending, suspending or revoking an airman certificate when —
options: The public interest and aviation safety require it
The certificate is more than five years old
questions.explain: GACAR §13.103 allows the President to issue an order amending, suspending or revoking a certificate when the public interest and aviation safety require it.

banks.title: Aircraft, Equipment & Airworthiness
banks.desc: Oxygen, transponders, instrument tests, airworthiness directives and maintenance under GACAR Parts 91, 39 and 43.

questions.q: On a non-pressurized aircraft, above which cabin pressure altitude must the required minimum flight crew use supplemental oxygen for the entire flight time at those altitudes?
questions.explain: GACAR §91.223 requires the minimum flight crew to use supplemental oxygen during the entire flight time at cabin pressure altitudes above 13,000 ft MSL.

questions.q: Between cabin pressure altitudes above 10,000 ft and up to and including 13,000 ft MSL, the required minimum flight crew must use supplemental oxygen for that part of the flight at those altitudes that lasts more than —
questions.explain: GACAR §91.223 requires supplemental oxygen for the minimum flight crew when the flight above 10,000 ft up to 13,000 ft MSL exceeds 30 minutes duration.

questions.q: Within what period must an ATC transponder have been tested and inspected for it to be used as required by GACAR Part 91?
questions.explain: GACAR §91.453 prohibits use of an ATC transponder unless it has been tested and inspected within the preceding 24 months and found to comply with Appendix E to GACAR Part 43.

questions.q: To operate an aircraft in controlled airspace under IFR, the altimeter and static pressure system must have been tested and inspected within —
questions.explain: GACAR §91.451 requires each static pressure system, altimeter and automatic pressure altitude reporting system to have been tested within the preceding 24 months to operate in controlled airspace under IFR.

questions.q: Generally, no person may operate an aircraft unless within the preceding 12 months it has had —
options: An annual inspection in accordance with GACAR Part 43
questions.explain: GACAR §91.449 prohibits operating an aircraft unless within the preceding 12 months it has had an annual inspection in accordance with GACAR Part 43 and been approved for return to service.

questions.q: Who is primarily responsible for maintaining an aircraft in an airworthy condition, including compliance with GACAR Part 39?
options: The owner or operator of the aircraft
questions.explain: GACAR §91.443 states the owner or operator of an aircraft is primarily responsible for maintaining it in an airworthy condition, including compliance with GACAR Part 39.

questions.q: Under GACAR Part 39, a person may operate an aircraft to which an airworthiness directive applies —
options: Only in accordance with the requirements of that airworthiness directive
questions.explain: GACAR §39.3 prohibits operating an aircraft to which an airworthiness directive applies except in accordance with the requirements of that directive.

questions.q: If there is a conflict between an airworthiness directive and a related service document, the affected person must —
questions.explain: GACAR §39.7 requires that, in the event of conflict between a service document and an airworthiness directive, the affected person must comply with the airworthiness directive.

questions.q: Under GACAR Part 39, a special flight permit may be issued for an aircraft to —
options: Carry passengers for hire despite an open AD
Fly to a repair facility to complete work required by an airworthiness directive, provided it is capable of safe flight
Operate indefinitely with an unsafe condition
questions.explain: GACAR §39.15 allows a special flight permit for an aircraft to fly to a repair facility to complete work required by an AD, provided the aircraft is capable of safe flight.

questions.q: Under GACAR §43.5, the holder of a pilot certificate (other than a sport pilot) may perform which work on an aircraft they own or operate and not used under Part 121 or 135?
questions.explain: GACAR §43.5(g) allows a pilot certificate holder (except a sport pilot) to perform preventive maintenance on an aircraft owned or operated by that pilot and not used under Part 121 or 135.

questions.q: Under GACAR §43.3, an aircraft component may be described as 'rebuilt' only if it has been disassembled, cleaned, inspected, repaired, reassembled and tested —
options: To any standard acceptable to the owner
To the same tolerances and limits as a new item
questions.explain: GACAR §43.3 permits describing an item as rebuilt only if it has been disassembled, cleaned, inspected, repaired, reassembled and tested to the same tolerances and limits as a new item.

questions.q: GACAR §43.19 requires that work done in maintenance, alteration or preventive maintenance leaves the item in a condition —
options: At least equal to its original or properly altered condition
Better than new in all respects
Acceptable to the aircraft owner only
Equal to the minimum equipment list standard
questions.explain: GACAR §43.19 requires the work to be done so the condition of the item is at least equal to its original or properly altered condition with regard to airworthiness.

questions.q: Which instruments are required for VFR day flight?
options: Airspeed indicator, altimeter and magnetic compass
questions.explain: Basic VFR-day equipment under GACAR Part 91 includes an airspeed indicator, altimeter and magnetic compass.

questions.q: An ELT is:
questions.explain: An ELT (Emergency Locator Transmitter) transmits a distress signal to aid search and rescue.

questions.q: Rules for maintenance and return to service are found in GACAR:
questions.explain: Maintenance, preventive maintenance and return to service are governed by GACAR Part 43.

questions.q: The pitot-static system supplies:
options: The airspeed indicator, altimeter and vertical speed indicator
questions.explain: The pitot-static system drives the airspeed indicator, altimeter and VSI.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: A blocked pitot tube (with the static port clear) primarily affects the:
questions.explain: A blocked pitot tube directly affects the airspeed indicator.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Continued airworthiness of an aircraft is maintained by:
options: Completing the required inspections and maintenance
questions.explain: Airworthiness is maintained by the inspections and maintenance required under GACAR Part 43.

banks.title: Aviation Weather
banks.desc: Atmosphere, clouds, fronts, thunderstorms, icing and visibility hazards drawn from the FAA Aviation Weather Handbook.

questions.q: What type of cloud is associated with severe turbulence, icing, lightning and hail, and should be avoided?
questions.explain: The FAA Aviation Weather Handbook identifies cumulonimbus clouds as thunderstorm clouds carrying severe turbulence, icing, lightning and hail - all thunderstorms are hazardous to aircraft.

questions.q: A thunderstorm cell transitions to the mature stage when which event occurs?
options: The downdraft cuts off the moisture supply
questions.explain: The FAA Aviation Weather Handbook states a cell transitions to the mature stage when precipitation reaches the surface, creating a strong downdraft alongside the updraft; weather hazards peak toward the end of this stage.

questions.q: In the International Standard Atmosphere, what are the sea level temperature and pressure?
questions.explain: The FAA Aviation Weather Handbook gives the standard atmosphere as 15 degrees C (59 degrees F) and 29.92 inches of mercury (1013.25 hPa) at sea level.

questions.q: What is the standard temperature lapse rate in the troposphere?
questions.explain: The FAA Aviation Weather Handbook lists the standard tropospheric lapse rate as 6.5 degrees C per 1,000 metres, which is about 3.57 degrees F - roughly 2 degrees C per 1,000 feet.

questions.q: Which surface weather measurement is most useful for anticipating the formation of fog?
questions.explain: The FAA Aviation Weather Handbook notes the surface temperature-dewpoint spread is important in anticipating fog; when the spread reaches zero the air is saturated and fog can form.

questions.q: Radiation fog forms primarily under which conditions?
options: Strong winds and overcast skies over water
Clear skies, light winds and moist air over land at night
Warm air moving over a cold sea surface
Air being forced up sloping terrain in daytime
questions.explain: Per the FAA Aviation Weather Handbook, radiation fog is favoured by a shallow moist surface layer, clear skies and light surface winds; it is a nighttime land occurrence caused by terrestrial cooling.

questions.q: How does advection fog form?
options: Moist air moving over a colder surface and cooling below its dewpoint
Nighttime radiational cooling of the ground
Rain evaporating into cold air beneath a front
Air being lifted over a mountain ridge
questions.explain: The FAA Aviation Weather Handbook defines advection fog as forming when moist air moves over a colder surface and is cooled below its dewpoint; it is common along coasts and is called sea fog at sea.

questions.q: A microburst is best described as which type of weather hazard?
options: A small-scale, intense downdraft - the most severe type of wind shear
A persistent layer of stratus cloud
questions.explain: The FAA Aviation Weather Handbook describes a microburst as a small-scale, intense downdraft that spreads outward at the surface and is the most severe type of wind shear, with downdrafts up to 6,000 fpm.

questions.q: Which structural icing type is rough, milky and opaque, and is the most frequently reported icing type?
questions.explain: The FAA Aviation Weather Handbook describes rime ice as rough, milky and opaque ice formed by instantaneous freezing of small supercooled droplets, and notes it is the most frequently reported icing type.

questions.q: Which force, caused by the Earth's rotation, deflects moving air to the right in the Northern Hemisphere?
questions.explain: The FAA Aviation Weather Handbook explains the Coriolis force is an apparent force from the Earth's rotation that deflects air to the right in the Northern Hemisphere, acting at a right angle to wind direction.

questions.q: What effect does stable air generally have on the vertical motion of air?
options: It has no effect on vertical motion
questions.explain: The FAA Aviation Weather Handbook states atmospheric stability affects vertical motion: stable air suppresses vertical motion, while unstable air enhances it.

questions.q: The arc-shaped leading edge of cool, gusty downdraft air spreading out ahead of a mature thunderstorm is called what?
questions.explain: The FAA Aviation Weather Handbook calls the arc-shaped leading edge of downdraft air a gust front; uplift along it can trigger new cells ahead of the parent cell.

questions.q: A sea breeze typically blows:
options: From land to sea by day
From sea to land by day
questions.explain: By day the land heats faster than the sea, so the surface wind (sea breeze) blows from sea to land.

questions.q: Fog forms when:
options: Air is heated above its dew point
Air is cooled to its dew point
questions.explain: Fog forms when air is cooled to its dew point and water vapour condenses near the surface.

questions.q: Structural icing requires:
options: Visible moisture and a temperature at or below freezing
questions.explain: Airframe icing needs visible moisture and a temperature at or below 0°C.

questions.q: A temperature inversion is a layer in which:
questions.explain: In an inversion, temperature increases with altitude, often trapping haze and stabilising the air.

questions.q: Wind shear is:
options: A sudden change in wind speed or direction over a short distance
questions.explain: Wind shear is a rapid change in wind velocity over a small change in position, hazardous near the ground.

questions.q: A METAR is:
options: A forecast for the next 24 hours
A routine, observed aviation weather report
questions.explain: A METAR is a routine observation of current conditions at an aerodrome (a TAF is the forecast).

banks.title: Navigation & Flight Planning
banks.desc: Charts, the coordinate system, magnetic variation and deviation, VOR navigation and wind correction from the FAA Pilot's Handbook.

questions.q: Lines running east and west, parallel to the equator and used to measure position north or south, are called what?
questions.explain: The FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge states circles parallel to the equator are parallels of latitude, used to measure degrees of latitude north or south of the equator.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: From which location is longitude measured as the zero (Prime) meridian?
questions.explain: The FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge states the Prime Meridian, which passes through Greenwich, England, is the zero line from which longitude is measured east and west to 180 degrees.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: In aerial navigation, what is the angular difference between true direction and magnetic direction called?
questions.explain: The FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge states the difference between true and magnetic directions is called variation; isogonic lines join points of equal variation.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: A line along which there is zero magnetic variation is known as what?
questions.explain: The FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge explains that along the agonic line the two poles are aligned and there is no variation.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Compass deviation is an error caused by what?
options: The difference between true and magnetic north
The aircraft's own localised magnetic influences acting on the compass
Wind drifting the aircraft off course
questions.explain: The FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge states each aircraft has internal magnetic influences affecting its compass; deviation is corrected using a compass deviation card to obtain the compass course.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Navigating by reference to visible landmarks on the ground is known as what?
questions.explain: The FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge defines pilotage as navigating by reference to visible landmarks, distinct from dead reckoning and radio navigation.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Dead reckoning is best described as which method of navigation?
options: Computing direction and distance from a known position
questions.explain: The FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge defines dead reckoning as navigation by computations of direction and distance from a known position.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: A VOR ground station projects courses outward in all directions; these courses are referred to as what?
questions.explain: The FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge explains a VOR projects straight-line courses called radials in all directions, identified by numbers from 001 to 360 and referenced to magnetic north.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Because VOR transmits on VHF, its signal reception is subject to what limitation?
options: It is restricted to line-of-sight, so range varies with altitude
questions.explain: The FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge notes VOR signals are VHF and therefore subject to line-of-sight restrictions, so reception range varies in direct proportion to the aircraft's altitude.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: The wind correction angle is applied to do what?
options: Counteract wind drift so the aircraft's track matches the desired course
questions.explain: The FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge explains the wind correction angle is the number of degrees the heading is offset from the true course to counteract wind drift and keep the track on the desired course.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: What is the scale of a standard sectional aeronautical chart?
questions.explain: The FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge states sectional charts have a scale of 1:500,000 (1 inch = about 6.86 NM), allowing detailed information to be shown.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Groundspeed differs from true airspeed in what way?
options: Groundspeed is true airspeed adjusted for wind
Groundspeed is always higher than true airspeed
Groundspeed is true airspeed corrected for temperature
Groundspeed and true airspeed are always equal
questions.explain: The FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge defines groundspeed as the actual speed over the ground - true airspeed adjusted for wind; it decreases with a headwind and increases with a tailwind.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Magnetic variation is the angle between:
questions.explain: Variation is the angular difference between true north and magnetic north at a location.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Compass deviation is caused by:
questions.explain: Deviation is the compass error produced by the aircraft’s own electrical and metallic components.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: One degree of latitude equals approximately:
questions.explain: One degree of latitude is about 60 nautical miles (1 minute ≈ 1 NM).
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: A VOR provides the pilot with:
questions.explain: A VOR gives bearing information as radials from the station; DME adds distance.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Isogonic lines on a chart connect points of equal:
questions.explain: Isogonic lines join points of equal magnetic variation (an agonic line has zero variation).
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Dead-reckoning navigation estimates position using:
questions.explain: Dead reckoning computes position from a known start using heading, speed, wind and elapsed time.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

banks.title: Flight Planning & Fuel
banks.desc: Mass & balance, fuel reserves, performance and the ISA — the planning numbers.
banks.source: GACAR Part 91 · FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: On a standard day (ISA), what is the sea-level temperature?
questions.explain: The ICAO Standard Atmosphere defines sea level as 15°C and 1013.25 hPa.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: What is the approximate ISA temperature lapse rate in the troposphere?
questions.explain: Temperature falls about 2°C (1.98°C) per 1,000 ft up to the tropopause.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: How does a high density altitude affect take-off performance?
questions.explain: Thinner air reduces engine thrust and lift, lengthening the take-off roll and flattening the climb.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Loading an aircraft aft of its centre-of-gravity limit mainly affects what?
questions.explain: An aft CG reduces longitudinal stability and can make stall recovery difficult.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Final reserve fuel is best described as fuel that…
options: may be used during the cruise
should never be planned for use
questions.explain: Final reserve is a protected minimum on landing — it is never planned to be consumed en route.

questions.q: One nautical mile is approximately how many metres?
questions.explain: By definition 1 NM = 1,852 m (1,609 m is a statute mile).
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: With a true airspeed of 120 kt and a 20 kt direct headwind, the groundspeed is…
questions.explain: A headwind subtracts from TAS: 120 − 20 = 100 kt groundspeed.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: To convert a fuel volume in litres to a mass, you multiply by its…
questions.explain: Mass = volume × density. Avgas density is roughly 0.72 kg/L.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: A stronger headwind component has what effect on take-off and landing distance?
questions.explain: A headwind reduces the ground run required for both take-off and landing.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Density altitude equals pressure altitude when…
options: the temperature equals ISA for that level
questions.explain: When the actual temperature matches the ISA value for that level, density altitude equals pressure altitude.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Pressure altitude is the altitude read when the altimeter sub-scale is set to:
questions.explain: Pressure altitude is indicated when the altimeter is set to the standard 1013.25 hPa / 29.92 inHg.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: The crosswind component is greatest when the wind is:
questions.explain: A wind at 90° to the runway is entirely crosswind; aligned with the runway it is all headwind.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Increasing aircraft weight will:
questions.explain: Greater weight increases both take-off and landing distance and raises the stall speed.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: On a hot, high-elevation aerodrome, density altitude is:
questions.explain: High temperature raises density altitude above pressure altitude, degrading performance.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: A VFR flight must plan to carry fuel to the destination plus:
questions.explain: Fuel planning must include trip fuel plus the required reserves under GACAR Part 91.

questions.q: Contingency (or extra) fuel is carried to allow for:
options: Unforeseen factors such as wind or re-routing
questions.explain: Contingency fuel covers unforeseen deviations from the plan, such as stronger winds or re-routing.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: For a fixed indicated airspeed, true airspeed:
questions.explain: As air density falls with altitude, true airspeed is higher than indicated airspeed for the same IAS.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: A wind correction angle is applied to the:
options: Heading, to maintain the desired track
questions.explain: The pilot turns the heading into wind by the wind correction angle to track the desired course.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

banks.title: Human Factors & Aeromedical
banks.desc: Hypoxia, carbon monoxide, spatial disorientation, fatigue and aeronautical decision-making from FAA aeromedical and risk-management references.

questions.q: Oxygen starvation from hypoxia affects which part of the body first?
questions.explain: The FAA Hypoxia brochure states oxygen starvation first affects the brain so judgment is impaired - a pilot may not realise they are in trouble.

questions.q: At 18,000 feet MSL, how does the available oxygen compare with sea level?
options: It is the same as at sea level
There is about half the oxygen of sea level
There is about one-quarter the oxygen of sea level
There is more oxygen than at sea level
questions.explain: The FAA Hypoxia brochure notes that at 18,000 feet MSL the air pressure is half that at sea level, so only half the oxygen is available.

questions.q: If a pilot detects an unusual cabin smell suggesting carbon monoxide, what is an appropriate immediate action?
options: Turn the cabin heat fully off and increase fresh-air ventilation
Increase cabin heat to clear the air
Ignore it and continue the flight
questions.explain: The FAA Carbon Monoxide brochure advises pilots to turn cabin heat fully off, increase fresh-air ventilation to maximum, consider supplemental oxygen, and land as promptly as possible.

questions.q: A 'black-hole approach' visual illusion is most likely to occur in which situation?
options: A daytime approach over a city
A night approach over water or unlit terrain to a lighted runway with no visible horizon
questions.explain: The FAA Spatial Disorientation brochure describes the black-hole approach illusion as occurring on a night final approach over water or unlighted terrain to a lighted runway beyond which the horizon is not visible.

questions.q: If a pilot experiences a visual illusion or spatial disorientation in flight, what is the recommended response?
options: Trust bodily sensations over the instruments
Have confidence in the flight instruments and ignore conflicting bodily signals
Close the eyes briefly to reset perception
questions.explain: The FAA Spatial Disorientation brochure advises pilots to have confidence in their instruments and ignore conflicting bodily signals, noting accidents usually result from indecision to rely on instruments.

questions.q: Which type of photoreceptor in the eye is used for low-light and night vision?
questions.explain: The FAA Pilot Vision brochure states rods are used for low light intensity or night vision, while cones handle colour and detail in brighter light.

questions.q: For effective visual scanning to detect other traffic, each eye movement should be limited to roughly what arc, observing each area for at least one second?
questions.explain: The FAA Pilot Vision brochure recommends a series of short, regularly-spaced eye movements not exceeding 10 degrees, with each area observed for at least one second to enable detection.

questions.q: Altitude-induced decompression sickness is caused by which mechanism?
options: Nitrogen coming out of solution and forming bubbles in the body
questions.explain: The FAA Altitude-Induced Decompression Sickness brochure explains that with reduced pressure, dissolved nitrogen can come out of solution and form bubbles in the body - the condition known as 'the bends'.

questions.q: Why must a pilot allow sufficient time after SCUBA diving before flying?
options: Excess nitrogen stored in the body can trigger decompression sickness at altitudes as low as 5,000 ft
Diving reduces visual acuity for several days
Flying too soon causes carbon monoxide poisoning
questions.explain: The FAA Altitude-Induced Decompression Sickness brochure warns that if not enough time is allowed to eliminate excess body nitrogen after diving, altitude DCS can occur at altitudes as low as 5,000 ft or less.

questions.q: What does the IMSAFE checklist help a pilot assess?
options: Personal fitness for flight - Illness, Medication, Stress, Alcohol, Fatigue and Emotion
questions.explain: The FAA Risk Management Handbook describes IMSAFE as an acronym for Illness, Medication, Stress, Alcohol, Fatigue and Emotion, used by a pilot to identify personal hazards before flight.

questions.q: Which of the following is one of the five hazardous attitudes identified in the FAA Risk Management Handbook?
questions.explain: The FAA Risk Management Handbook lists five hazardous attitudes: anti-authority, impulsivity, invulnerability ('It won't happen to me'), macho and resignation.

questions.q: The PAVE checklist is used by a pilot for what purpose?
options: Identifying hazards and managing risk before and during a flight
questions.explain: The FAA Risk Management Handbook presents the PAVE checklist as a tool for risk management analysis, helping the pilot identify hazards to decide on the desirability of undertaking or continuing a flight.

questions.q: The first action for a pilot experiencing hypoxia is to:
questions.explain: Hypoxia is treated by supplemental oxygen and descending to a lower altitude.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Hyperventilation is best corrected by:
questions.explain: Hyperventilation (over-breathing) is corrected by consciously slowing the breathing rate.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Spatial disorientation is best overcome by:
options: Trusting and relying on the flight instruments
questions.explain: When disoriented, the pilot should trust the flight instruments rather than bodily sensations.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: The "IM SAFE" checklist is used to assess:
options: The pilot’s personal fitness to fly
questions.explain: IM SAFE (Illness, Medication, Stress, Alcohol, Fatigue, Emotion/Eating) checks the pilot’s fitness to fly.

questions.q: Carbon monoxide poisoning in a light aircraft most often comes from:
questions.explain: CO can leak from a cracked exhaust into the cabin-heat system; a CO detector and fresh air are key defences.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Which is one of the five hazardous attitudes in aeronautical decision-making?
questions.explain: The five hazardous attitudes are anti-authority, impulsivity, invulnerability, macho and resignation.
